title: Shear Banding in Amorphous Solids as a Nonlinear Screened Soft Mode Instability
authors: Yang Fu, Yuliang Jin, Avanish Kumar, Itamar Procaccia
abstract: Shear banding is a well-known and widespread instability in strained solids: under external strain, the deformation localizes along a line in two dimensions or a plane in three dimensions. Developing a proper theoretical description of this phenomenon is key to understanding mechanical failure in solid materials. Very recently, a nonlinear theory extending classical elasticity to include plastic deformations as topological charges was proposed, offering detailed predictions on the nature and consequences of the shear-banding instability. The theory derives a Hessian operator whose lowest eigenvalue vanishes at the onset of instability, and the corresponding critical eigenmode describes the displacement field across the shear band. The resulting soft mode possesses the selected localization scale and subsequently saturates into a finite-width shear band. The aim of this Letter is to examine this theory numerically, establishing the role of topological screening and nonlinear instability as the mechanisms governing shear banding during athermal quasistatic deformation. We show that the displacement profile around the shear band is directly determined by the screening parameter and the nonlinear coefficient, thereby quantitatively verifying the theoretical predictions. Our results demonstrate that shear banding differs fundamentally from fracture: it arises from a nonlinear instability of an elastic field screened by plastic deformations. This establishes topological screening as the essential mechanism governing shear banding in amorphous solids.
summary: This work theoretically and numerically analyzes shear banding in amorphous solids, proposing that this mechanical failure mode is not a simple fracture but rather a nonlinear instability mediated by topological screening. By simulating atomic models under shear, the authors confirm that the finite width of the resulting shear band is quantitatively determined by this screening mechanism.
main_problem: The paper investigates shear banding, a mechanical instability in amorphous solids, by developing and numerically testing a nonlinear theory that incorporates plastic deformations as topological charges.
main_result: Shear banding is fundamentally established as a screened soft-mode instability, where the finite localization scale of the band is governed by topological screening rather than classical elasticity.
method: The authors combine theoretical derivation (using nonlinear Lagrangian methods) with extensive numerical simulations on atomic models to verify quantitative predictions for the shear band width and amplitude.
model_or_system: The study uses amorphous solids simulated via molecular dynamics using various repulsive potentials (harmonic, Hertzian, WCA) under athermal quasistatic shearing conditions.
key_observables: Accumulated stress ($\Sigma_{xy}$) vs. strain ($\gamma$), displacement profiles ($d_x(y)$), quadrupolar charge density ($Q$), and the screening parameter ($\kappa_e^2$).
important_parameters: The screening length ($\ell \sim 1/\kappa_e$) and nonlinear coefficients derived from the instability equations are crucial for characterizing the band.
main_assumptions: The analysis often relies on simplifying assumptions, such as fixing boundary conditions or using a 1D projection approximation for the displacement field to match theoretical models to simulations.
figures_summary: Figures show stress-strain curves exhibiting shear localization, spatial maps of charge densities concentrated in bands, and quantitative fits comparing simulated displacement profiles and screening parameters against theoretical predictions.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the general nonlinear theory for instability (soft mode analysis) to applying this framework numerically using atomic simulations across different potentials, culminating in a demonstration that topological screening dictates the finite width of the shear band.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter mechanics, the concept of an instability being governed by a 'screening' mechanism analogous to those studied in quantum field theory or plasma physics provides a rich analogy for understanding non-equilibrium phase transitions in complex many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
